title: Isaiah 44
subtitle: I Am the First and I Am the Last

sections.header: Verses 1–20 — “Apart from Me There Is No God”

heb.paragraph: "I am the first (riʾashōn) and I am the last; apart from me there is no God" (v.6). Revelation 1:17 and 22:13 apply this title to Christ: "I am the First and the Last." God pours water on the thirsty land and his Spirit on Israel’s offspring (v.3). "One will say, ‘I belong to the LORD’; another will call himself by the name of Jacob; still another will write on his hand, ‘The LORD’s’" (v.5). Then the devastating satire on idol-making (vv.9–20): a man plants a tree, it grows, he cuts half of it for firewood and cooks his meal, then from the other half he makes a god and bows down: "Save me! You are my god!" (v.17). "He feeds on ashes; a deluded heart misleads him" (v.20). The funniest and most savage idol-satire in the Bible.

notes.note: "I will pour water on the thirsty land, and streams on the dry ground; I will pour out my Spirit on your offspring." Water = Spirit. The Pentecost promise in Isaiah: 32:15 and now 44:3.
"I am the first and I am the last; apart from me there is no God." Rev 1:17; 22:13 give this title to Jesus. The absolute monotheism that the NT applies christologically.

"Half of the wood he burns in the fire; from the other half he makes a god. He prays to it and says, Save me! He feeds on ashes." The idol-satire: the same tree provides both firewood and a deity. The logic is deliberately absurd. Isaiah wants the audience to laugh before they think.

refs.note: Paul's Areopagus critique of idolatry as human projection parallels Isaiah's satire on the absurdity of worshiping what hands have made.
Paul's analysis of idolatry as exchanging the Creator's glory for images echoes Isaiah's polemic.
The psalm's idol satire — mouths that cannot speak, eyes that cannot see — shares Isaiah's mockery.
Jeremiah's scarecrow-in-a-melon-patch satire parallels Isaiah's craftsman critique: idols cannot walk, speak, or save.

hist.context: Isaiah 44:9–20 contains the most extended idol satire in the Hebrew Bible. A craftsman cuts a tree, uses half for fuel to warm himself and roast meat, then carves the other half into a god and bows down to it. The absurdity is deliberate: the same wood that becomes ash also becomes deity. The satire exposes idolatry as category confusion — worshiping what you make rather than the Maker. This is not mere polemic; it addresses exiles surrounded by Babylon's impressive cult, reassuring them that YHWH alone is God (vv.6–8).

sections.header: Verses 21–28 — “I Have Swept Away Your Offences Like a Cloud”

heb.paragraph: "I have swept away your offences like a cloud, your sins like the morning mist. Return to me, for I have redeemed you" (v.22). The creation-praise: "Sing for joy, you heavens! Shout, you earth! Burst into song, you mountains!" (v.23). Then the climax: "I am the LORD, who says of Jerusalem, ‘It shall be inhabited,’ who says of Cyrus (kōresh), ‘He is my shepherd and will accomplish all that I please’" (vv.26, 28). CYRUS NAMED for the first time — a pagan Persian king called God’s shepherd 150 years before he conquers Babylon (539 BC). The temple will be rebuilt (v.28). The naming of Cyrus is the most specific predictive prophecy in Second Isaiah.

notes.note: "I have swept away your offences like a cloud, your sins like the morning mist." Sins as cloud and mist: they existed, they obscured, and then the wind blew them away. Forgiveness as atmospheric clearance.
"Sing for joy, you heavens! Shout, you earth! Burst into song, you mountains!" The cosmic doxology: creation itself sings when God forgives. Redemption produces worship in nature.

"He says of Cyrus, ‘He is my shepherd, and will accomplish all that I please; he will say of Jerusalem, Let it be rebuilt.’" Cyrus named. A pagan king called God’s shepherd. The most historically specific prophecy in Second Isaiah.

refs.note: Cyrus's edict fulfills Isaiah's prophecy: the Persian king commands the temple to be rebuilt and releases the exiles.
The Chronicler's closing verses quote the Cyrus decree, showing Scripture's sense of prophetic fulfilment.
The explicit naming of Cyrus as YHWH's anointed (māšîaḥ) continues the Cyrus oracle begun at 44:28.
Jeremiah's seventy-year prophecy for Babylon's fall aligns with Isaiah's announcement of the city's rebuilding agent.

hist.context: The chapter concludes with the first explicit naming of Cyrus (v.28), called 'my shepherd' who will authorize Jerusalem's rebuilding and the temple's foundation. This is remarkable: a pagan king is YHWH's agent of restoration. The oracle is set roughly 550–540 BC, when Cyrus was conquering the Median empire and would soon topple Babylon (539 BC). Isaiah's claim that YHWH named Cyrus before his rise is central to the predictive-proof argument running through these chapters: only the true God can declare the end from the beginning.

ppl.name: king
ppl.text: king is a key figure in I Am the First and I Am the Last.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVWho then is like me? Let him proclaim it. Let him declare and lay out before me what has happened since I established my ancient people, and what is yet to come— yes, let them foretell what will come.ESVWho is like me? Let him proclaim it. Let him declare and set it before me, since I appointed an ancient people. Let them declare what is to come, and what will happen.NIVThis is what the Lord says— he who made you, who formed you in the womb, and who will help you: Do not be afraid, Jacob, my servant, Jeshurun, whom I have chosen.ESVThus says the LORD who made you, who formed you from the womb and will help you: Fear not, O Jacob my servant, Jeshurun whom I have chosen.

rec.text: ""I will pour water on the thirsty land, and streams on the dry ground; I will pour out my Spirit on your offspring." Water = Spirit. The Pentecost promise in Isaiah: 32:15 and now 44:3."

rows.label: Cyrus Named — 44:21–28
rows.text: "I have swept away your sins like a CLOUD." Heavens sing! "CYRUS is my shepherd" — named 150 years early.

hebtext.note: "I am the first (riʾashōn) and I am the last; apart from me there is no God" (v.6). Revelation 1:17 and 22:13 apply this title to Christ: "I am the First and the Last." God pours water on the thirsty land and his Spirit on Israel’s offspring (v.3). "One will say, ‘I belong to the LORD’; another will call himself by the name of Jacob; still another will write on his hand, ‘The LORD’s’" (v.5). Then the devastating satire on idol-making (vv.9–20): a man plants a tree, it grows, he cuts half of it for firewood and cooks his meal, then from the other half he makes a god and bows down: "Save me! You are my god!" (v.17). "He feeds on ashes; a deluded heart misleads him" (v.20). The funniest and most savage idol-satire in the Bible.

"I have swept away your offences like a cloud, your sins like the morning mist. Return to me, for I have redeemed you" (v.22). The creation-praise: "Sing for joy, you heavens! Shout, you earth! Burst into song, you mountains!" (v.23). Then the climax: "I am the LORD, who says of Jerusalem, ‘It shall be inhabited,’ who says of Cyrus (kōresh), ‘He is my shepherd and will accomplish all that I please’" (vv.26, 28). CYRUS NAMED for the first time — a pagan Persian king called God’s shepherd 150 years before he conquers Babylon (539 BC). The temple will be rebuilt (v.28). The naming of Cyrus is the most specific predictive prophecy in Second Isaiah.

themes.note: Prophecy peaks at 10: Cyrus named 150 years before his conquest. "First and Last" applied to Christ (Rev 1:17). Mercy at 9: sins swept like clouds. The idol-satire: the most savage and funny in the Bible.

coaching.tip: The idol-maker satire (vv. 9–20) is devastating comedy. A man cuts down a tree, burns half for warmth, cooks dinner over another portion, and carves the rest into a god he worships: 'Save me! You are my god!' (v. 17). Isaiah's method is ridicule, not argument. He doesn't refute idolatry philosophically — he makes it absurd. Laughter is his apologetic weapon.

'I have swept away your offences like a cloud, your sins like the morning mist' (v. 22). Sin is compared to fog — real, obscuring, but temporary. God's forgiveness doesn't negotiate with sin; it evaporates it. The meteorological metaphor suggests effortlessness: what took centuries to accumulate disappears like dawn mist. This is grace described in terms of physics rather than law.
